name: Dates

book_claims.claim: Traditional frugal food consumed at dusk during Ramadan fasting
book_claims.mechanism: Provides quick glucose replenishment after extended fast without large caloric load

supplementary_research.finding: Review (Al-Farsi & Lee, 2008; updated Vayalil, 2012) found dates contain high antioxidant capacity due to carotenoids, phenolics, and flavonoids, with anti-inflammatory and anti-tumour properties demonstrated in vitro.
Despite high sugar content (~65-80%), dates have a low-to-moderate glycaemic index (GI 42-55 depending on variety) due to their fibre and fructose content (Alkaabi et al., 2011; Rock et al., 2009).
RCT found date consumption did not adversely affect blood glucose or lipid profiles in healthy individuals, and the high potassium content may support blood pressure regulation (Rock et al., 2009).

culinary_pairings.source: Traditional pairing as stuffed dates; protein + fat slow glucose absorption
Spiced date preparations; cinnamon moderates glycaemic response
Dates with labneh; natural sweetener for dairy
Date-sweetened oatmeal and energy bars; whole food sugar replacement

seasonality: Harvested September-December. Dried dates available year-round. Medjool are the premium fresh-eating variety. Deglet Noor are drier and better for cooking.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: One of the richest fruit sources of potassium. Supports blood pressure regulation and electrolyte balance.
High fibre content partially explains lower-than-expected glycaemic index despite high sugar content.
Antioxidant capacity comparable to some berries. Higher in darker varieties.
Contributes to daily intake; supports glucose metabolism and muscle function.
High sugar content moderated by fibre matrix. GI 42-55 depending on variety -- lower than white bread.
